speaker: interviewer
text: Alex Chevion interview, take three. Mr. Chevion, you were in Russia--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you were going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I was going to school. It was a high school. Every ethnic group was entitled in Russia to be educated in its own ethnic language being the language of instruction. What it means is you studied mathematics, but the language of instruction was Yiddish in my case. And in particular, I remember the lessons of Yiddish and Yiddish literature. We had a teacher from Kiev. Now, Yiddish is a very peculiar language. Unlike any other language, it's basically German and Hebrew. It's a mixture of German Hebrew, mostly German. But it's a bastardized German. And this teacher taught us Yiddish grammar and the lesson went like that. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] For instance, a [INAUDIBLE]. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Now, what was funny about that, that here was an example of the Yiddish grammar which was supposed to prove that words which are pronounced as an [INAUDIBLE] are feminine order, gender. And all the four words were actually of Polish origin. [SPEAKING YIDDISH], they come from the Polish language. And that's how the Yiddish-- so I found it very funny. It was very difficult to concentrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we-- I remember we studied [INAUDIBLE], we studied Sholen Aleichem. Sholem Aleichem was made out to be a great Russian hero because his stories dealt with plain people like Tevye der Milkhiker. So is made up on the basis of Tevye der Milkhiker. It deals with a Jewish milkman, a poor man working very hard. So this background lent itself sort of to the glorification of the poor man. [INAUDIBLE] has a story, [INAUDIBLE], which deals with a-- a Jewish owner of flour mill which exploits his workers pretty much. [INAUDIBLE] is his employee and he's being exploited terribly. So this was also glorified. So I remember that very much. And of course, lessons in mathematics was-- and biology, in particular, was Yiddish as the language of instructions really funny, was really [? difficult. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, next after the Germans [? ran ?] over the Russian part, I found myself suddenly under Hitler. And my immediate decision was I go back to Tarnow because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your father and your brother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my brother-- my brother. And we were able to correspond because it was just one country. So the mails were going out. It took a week for every letter, two weeks for a letter to reach and be exchanged, but it was possible to communicate by letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother. So obviously, my mother had a job. The apartment which our apartment was divided into three apartments, three different families lived there, but she still had the living room, which was the major part and the kitchen. So the kitchen was shared by all the three families. And she had a job and we-- she believed that she can get work for us, too. The question was just how to get from there to Tarnow. And this was like a distance of 200 kilometers. Jews were not allowed to travel already by train, already at that time, by train or by bus or by this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in a way, that was a difficulty. But we managed to overcome this difficulty, kind of smuggled ourselves through-- back to Tarnow. And we got papers had to be arranged because the-- there was at that point-- Tarnow was ruled by Judenrat, which meant the Germans appointed a Jewish commission which sort of ruled the town. And they had their own police, Jewish policemen. So they didn't have weapons, but they had sticks. And we had to somehow get papers which would hide the fact that we were in Russia. Because as such, you know, the Germans-- every once in a while, the Germans would pick up somebody and shoot them for whatever infraction of the rules were.

speaker: interviewee
text: They terrorized the town that way. If there was any infraction, you'd be shot on the spot. I remember one day a girl which she went to the same school that I went, although we were not in the same class, she was just shot one day. She was taken out of the cemetery and the gestapo shot her for whatever reason. So the rules-- the laws were such that you practically didn't have any rights. And yet, most Jews were still surviving and believing and really, at that point it looked like they had a good chance to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived back in Tarnow. First we hid for two months until the papers were arranged and so forth. And the fact that we were in Russia was concealed in those papers, you know. In other words, the papers were so arranged as though they were issued right then when everybody else's papers were issued. And my-- my mother persuaded this Edwin Hauser, was now the owner of my father's factory, to give us jobs. So we had jobs and we worked. How did you get back? Physically how did you get back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it so happens, we paid a big bribe to a German who put us in his private car and transported us out from Lwow to-- and my mother knew somebody and arranged with somebody. He was simply doing it for the money. So we arrived and we stayed there. And we-- I lived in Tarnow from 1941 till 1942, June 22. Now, during that year I worked in [INAUDIBLE], that was the name of my father's factory. And everything seemed all right until about starting from May. The Tarnow ghetto was being enclosed by a fence. A wooden fence all around the ghetto was built.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto first set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was set up in-- in-- in March of 1942. The ghetto was two reasons. The ghetto existed from day one. The-- the Jews had to live in a certain quarter, but it wasn't fenced in. Starting in June-- in March of 1942, they started to fence in the ghetto and there was talk about resettling, which is referred now we call it [? accion ?] number one. Because there were three [? axes ?] in Tarnow called actions, but they're [? accion. ?] [? Accion ?] meant-- I don't know why they used that word. But these were basically resettlings where the Germans persuaded everybody and the Judenrat got the order to prepare lists and select certain people each time for that action. In the first action, they took mostly older people and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this, the date? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first action was sometimes at the end of June, because I left right before the first action-- right before that. And also they started to fence in the ghetto. The ghetto which existed, the ghetto existed only in the sense that the Judenrat and the Jewish police was ruling that. There was a number of streets which were designated only for Jews. The Jews who lived outside of that part had to leave and move into that part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the apartment that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside that ghetto because we lived in a Jewish neighborhood. So that apartment was inside that ghetto. So I lived there and the talk was about this resettlement, you see. And of course, people with jobs like we had, in a factory which was classified as number one importance-- there were all kinds of classifications like mostly three or four-- but because this factory worked for the Germans, supplying them what they needed for the-- they were building at the front, you know, improvised housing and so forth which needed to cover was the tar paper. So the tar paper was very critical.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that factory had this number one designation and anybody who worked there had papers that he worked for the number one designation so he was safe from this first resettlement. So I was fairly safe. It looked at that point that probably they would never touch me-- somebody like me because I was young and I was working. But I had a premonition at that point practically nobody else had. Nobody at the time knew. And very few Jews at that point left to do what I did, which is on one Saturday, I took off my yellow star, which I had here-- everybody had to wear a yellow star. When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started in 1939, right when the Germans arrived. Right from day one, everybody had to have a yellow star. That was the first order of business, you see. So I just took off to yellow star and right-- went to the railroad station. I didn't have any documents at that point yet, I didn't have any papers. I went to the railroad station, bought a ticket for Lwow and went to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had just come from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's a funny story. But anyhow, I went and I made this decision, I'm starting on from now on, I'm not-- I pretend I'm not a Jew. But what I remember is the night before, when I said the final goodbye to my mother-- because for the whole week we were talking about this-- she cried. She begged me not to do that. My brother was just telling me that you're-- you're an idiot. You'll get caught 55,000 times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here you have a job and we all survived the war. Just suffer a little bit. Big deal, so we are slave laborers. So who cares? Another year and that's it. How long will Hitler last? Another two years, you know. And I keep telling them what I believed was going to happen to all of them. And what got me so upset is that about two weeks before I departed, some of the German soldiers who escorted us to the factory-- the factory was like five miles away from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, we would be-- all gather together and go with some transportation, horse and buggy, taken to the factory and so forth. But suddenly, German soldiers would take us. And I couldn't understand why, you know. And then the ghetto was being fenced in. And this was part of the reason why the German soldiers were coming. Suddenly, nobody could enter, leave or out unless he's escorted by a German soldier. And also, going to work meant gathering in-- in-- in a-- in a yard, which was, you know, right there like a central place where all-- all the people who went to work were gathered in that yard and they were sorted out in groups and each group was then escorted by these German soldiers. And most people took it not a big deal. But I had this premonition, you know. And, indeed, because I left when I left, it was so easy. Nobody suspected at that point. Nobody heard of any Jew pretending to be a Gentile. It was unheard of yet. Later it became often very popular and everybody was suspected of doing that, you see. When my parents left, my mother and my brother did that three months later, but they left after the third action, after the third resettlement so no Jews were left subsequently, you know. So everybody suspected and word spread around that many Jews are trying to save themselves by tearing off the yellow star and simply pretending that they're not Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So having left when I left made it so much easier for me. So that was June 22, I remember as the day, Saturday. I went to the railroad station. What struck me really at the railroad station was a bunch of people trying to enter the trains and there was not enough place for everybody, so there was a big crowd pushing into that small door and everything. And suddenly, German soldiers are coming and beating up everybody else. And that kind of made me feel safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suddenly thought that the Poles are, you know, being treated like-- like-- like, you know, dog, you know. So I kind of felt-- I don't know why, that gave me security here I'm running for my life and if somebody uncovers me, I can be-- but I felt that I'm not only-- the only one. In a way, everybody is sort of running away from the Germans or being hit or beaten or so forth, you know. And I entered the train, the train was packed full, you could hardly move there, and I arrived in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we walk out past that, in Tarnow--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how was life actually in the ghetto? Was there enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- there was two kinds, people who had money and could use gold or diamonds or whatever and change it. A little gold went a long, long way. In other words, if you sold something for a gram of gold, you got an awful lot of money. And my mother would be selling from her jewelry, which she had quite a bit, because Jews generally believed in accumulating jewelery in-- in those years because of the tradition of having to run, starting from [INAUDIBLE]. The history was always full of all kinds of pograms and runnings. They developed the sense that they need to-- to hoard gold and dollars, you know. And my mother had money, which you know, my father actually left it when he left. So that helped her. And if you had money, you could buy food. There was a black market for everything, you see. Plus, the fact that my mother worked as a cook helped her also to get some provisions which she could bring home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was not hungry, not hungry. Clothes, you couldn't buy clothes. There was no new clothes available. You had to live with your old clothes, you know. What about the role of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat, it depends in which town. Basically, they were collaborators in a way, but you had to say-- you know, there is a big controversy of the issue of the Judenrat which I don't want to dwell in. But you might have heard about Hannah Arendt. Hanna Arendt was a philosopher, a renowned philosopher in the United States, a professor at Columbia and all that, many publications. She declared all the Judenrats to be traitors and collaborators of the worst kind. So that's her opinion. She might be right, she might not be right. On the other hand, the people in the Judenrat claimed, look, under the circumstances, we did some good. Now, the kind of good they did obviously was of the nature that they selected the people who were resettled. So if they selected, say, older people, they clearly helped [INAUDIBLE] younger people. I-- I don't know how to react to that or how judge that, you know. But they claimed that if it weren't for them, things would have been much worse. Their function was usually A, to enforce order. They had the full authority as far as the Jews were concerned. B, the Germans would every once in a while say, you have to collect $10,000, you have to collect the kilogram of gold or whatever, and they would extract that money from-- from the people, you know, and deliver it to the Germans. And that would stave off the day of reckoning and so forth. But people not in large numbers were constantly killed just on the spot by any German gestapo men who would arrive in the ghetto. They did that just to spread fear, to keep the people scared all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see, but you heard of it. I know for-- know intimately about this case of-- of [? Bella ?] [? Weisberg, ?] who was a friend of mine was a girl who was very beautiful. And suddenly a German came into just the square there and picks her up and takes her and shoots her. And that's one case, you know. You heard of cases like this all the time, somebody being shot here, somebody being shot there. They would arrive in a car, round up whoever they saw, kill, kill, kill, and all that. So that was mostly to spread fear, you know, and maybe their savage pleasure. But all of this until the-- the official resettlement and Holocaust started in Auschwitz and amounted maybe, a town like Tarnow, I would guess at the most over the three years that there were 1,000 people, more like 500, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you get on a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you think you were going to be able to conceal your Jewish identity without papers, without anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was one incident, though, which prompted me mostly to do that. About, oh, a month or three weeks before I left, this German who owned my father's factory had with him two girls who were his concubines or lovers or whatever-- not officially married to them, but they both lived with him. And one day, one of those walks in into-- I worked in a place which was foundry and a repair shop together, you know. And in other words, they had horses there-- my father had a lot of horses and carts also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this place repaired the carts and provided, you know, for horses for what do you call it-- Horseshoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Horseshoes, horseshoes for the horse. And I worked in that. And also, every once in a while I would be called upon to fit a key for somebody. This woman, his secretary, walks in one day, gives me a key, and tells me to fit a key for her. Now, this was done by hand, unlike here that you do it with a machine, you copy off a machine. You had to take the two keys and with a file work very closely and do it by hand. I did that and I missed. It's enough you miss one-- the key didn't fit. She comes the next thing to the place and she had such a leather-- every German was walking with such a leather strip-- strap. And she straps with it, hits me over-- straight over my face and throws the key and tells me, you better make that key fit or else you will pay with you life. So of course, I was helped at that point and double checked that the key should fit and all that. But then at lunchtime, I sat down and with one of the guys-- a plain, very plain spoken person, you know, who still worked for my father from years before.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sat down there and talked to each other and he tells me, I would not put up with something like that. So I left and I thought, [INAUDIBLE] so what will you do? So he said, I would run into the forest. So I said, how will you live in the forest and so forth. So we got to talking, you know. And suddenly, the fact that she hit me like that and what he was kept telling me-- and in the process of this conversation, he also told me that in his village there is a man by the name of [INAUDIBLE], who went in 1939 to the war and never came back. And his mother doesn't know till today what happened to him. There is no letter, no word, no nothing. And I asked him, could you go to the priest and somehow ask him for a birth certificate? And he said, yes, he could go to the priest and ask for a birth certificate. And he gave me that-- he brought me that paper, you see. So all I had is a birth certificate and a false name-- no picture, no identity. Because no-- by the way, at that point, the Poles did not have identity cards which they were issued subsequently. So I left with this birth certificate and largely out of that incident, you know, plus the fact that the German soldiers were suddenly escorting us to work. And I had this premonition that this is the start of something to come, you know. I left. And that's-- that's how I started this career and in a way paved the way for my brother, who three months later followed me while at that point he was still ridiculing me. My mother was crying when I left.

speaker: interviewee
text: She felt that she's losing me forever because within days I would be arrested and, you know, hung or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, did you still have the radio that you spoke of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. God forbid. For radio, you would be shot. Nobody in the ghetto had a radio-- nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was news, there were rumors. And people were somehow, I would say, pretty much informed. But you see, in 1942, Hitler was still not losing. The turn came at Stalingrad, and that was still before Stalingrad. Later on, when I was in Warsaw already, and I was still well-informed because you could buy a newspaper which was an official German newspaper but it had an insert which the underground had put into that. And this is how I-- I wold be informed. And I knew pretty well about Stalingrad and all that. I also learned, like most people learned, to listen to the German radio which you couldn't listen at home. But if you would go in public places, the German radio would blast the news over the radio publicly for the population to listen. You learned to listen to their news and day from day to somehow figure out where they are lying, because everything was lies. But you figured it out in sort of like a, you know, you developed a method where you could guess due to the fact that they were saying one day this and the other day that, gee, there must be some big trouble now at, you now, Stalingrad and all that. And together with that newspaper, people were informed during the war-- not up to date, but they were informed. So But not in that time-- when I left in 1942, Hitler looked very victorious, very victorious. This was just a year after France and Hitler marched into Paris. And the British were-- were running away, you know, in-- in I forgot the name of the town, [INAUDIBLE] or whatever the name was, where the whole British fleet was running for their life. He was so powerful. And then in 1941, when he attacked Russia, Russia crumbled. Russia was supposed to be a power, you know, and they-- they crumbled. So-- so that, in 1942, although I didn't doubt that he would lose the war because if you doubted that he'd lose the war, you might as well stop living, you know. You had to believe. But there were no actual signs of the fact of his impending defeat. A year later, it became very clear after Stalingrad that it will be different. Stalingrad was then turn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Lwow. It was June--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Went to Lwow June 1942. The reason I went to Lwow because from correspondence with my father-- who couldn't, by the way, come back to town for the reason that before the war, he hired a German expert who was supposed to provide some improved efficiency in his manufacturing process. Later on, he had to fire that German expert. They didn't get along and so forth. After the war, that Germany appeared and asked my mother where my father is. He was looking for my father. Therefore, my father could never come back to Tarnow because he was afraid that this guy is after him, you know. Now, my father was in Lwow and he managed to get there a job in a factory, similar factory, like he had before the war. He was doing more or less the same thing. He had a close friend there, a Gentile, close friend, who was willing to give me housing. And I knew that from letters from my father. So I came, I arrived at my father's factor and that the guy, who was my father's friend, agreed to let me live in his house for a while. It was like I sublet, a rental.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your ID ever checked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that point, people didn't have IDs. And if I had been checked, I don't know if I would have withstood the questioning. So I was lucky. I learned to avoid being-- you know, I developed a sense-- the Germans would round up Poles occasionally and catch them and send them to Germany or just interrogate them and all that. And I make it my business to avoid being caught in situations like this because [? if I ?] had, it would have been very bad. But in about-- I was-- that was June. In September, the Germans suddenly announced that every Pole has to have an identity card. And the way to get at this, you submit your birth certificate and you fill out a form and then you come and pick up the identity card from the police. And I did what every other Pole did. I submitted my birth certificate, I filled out the form, and I came and I picked up my identity card. And I was one of the very few Jews, because later I met many Jews, including my brother, who lived on these Aryan papers, so to speak. But they had all forged papers, which looked very closely to the-- they were good forgeries, you know. However, if their papers would have been checked in the police for [? authenticity, ?] they would have failed at that check. Mine, at least I knew that it's like a legitimate document, that it will pass. Still, though, I was in one danger. If they asked me to drop my pants, which they did-- as times went on, it became very popular to ask people just on the mere suspicion, even if they were not Jews, drop your pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were brought into the police station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Or sometimes they would even didn't take them to the side of the street, drop your pants. And they would observe if the person was showing that he scared or hesitant and wouldn't drop his pants immediately, right there he was suspect, you know. So that became a big issue. And really, it became a big problem. And in May of 1943, I managed-- we managed to find a surgeon who was willing to operate and make the penis look as though it was not circumcised. Unfortunately, the operation did not succeed in most cases, except of one person that I know who was in his fifties. Because one requirement for the success was no erection for a period of two weeks, which for young people is-- you know, when a person's 20 years of age, it's almost impossible, you know. They have these nocturnal whatever stimulations and so-- but even so, we did not succeed. But to some extent, it had the scars, which would indicate that this person maybe was not circumcised but underwent some operations due to some venereal disease or so forth. It was something which might have helped. And, indeed, later on if we get to this when I tell you about this story in Romania, I was examined by doctors who had big doubts whether I was circumcised.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop now and continue in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.
